steps.title: Welcome to Day 10
steps.body: You have templates. You have workflows. Today you learn automation — setting things up so AI does the routine work without you having to think about it.

steps.title: What automation looks like
steps.body: Every morning you get 50+ emails. You spend 45 minutes sorting them. With automation, AI can summarize the inbox, flag urgent ones, draft replies to the routine ones, and leave you with only the 3 emails that actually need your brain.

steps.title: The automation mindset
steps.body: Ask yourself: 'Am I doing the same thing more than 3 times?' If yes, automate it. Not with code — with AI prompts you have already designed. The template from Day 8? That is automation.
steps.primaryButton: I have been automating without knowing it

steps.title: Automation prompt
steps.prompt: Every Monday I receive a list of tasks from my manager. Here is a sample: [paste sample]. Create a fill-in-the-blank prompt I can use every Monday to organize these into priority order, deadlines, and blocked items.

steps.title: Spotting automation
steps.question: Which of these is a good candidate for AI automation?
options: Replying to a customer who asks the same question every week
Having a deep conversation with your partner
feedback: Strategy needs your human judgment — use AI for research, but not the plan itself.
Exactly. Repetitive tasks are what automation is made for.
Some conversations are best kept entirely human.
Moral decisions should stay with you.

steps.title: Automate your routine

fields.label: What routine task do you do at least once a week?
fields.placeholder: reading and replying to status update emails

fields.label: What is the most tedious part?
fields.placeholder: writing the same update over and over

fields.label: What triggers this task?
fields.placeholder: I get an email with subject 'Weekly Status'

steps.template: I have a weekly task: {{repeat_task}}. The tedious part is {{pain_point}}. It is triggered by {{trigger}}. Design a reusable AI automation that handles this for me.

steps.title: Save your automation
steps.body: Paste the automation AI designed for you. This is your weekly time-saver.

steps.title: The 80/20 rule of automation
steps.body: Do not aim for 100% automation. Aim for 80%. Let AI handle the bulk, review the output, and make adjustments. 80% automated, 20% you is the sweet spot.

steps.title: True or false?
steps.question: AI automation should aim to replace all of your work so you never have to check anything.
feedback: Automation is a helper, not a replacement. You always review.
Right. The goal is to save time, not hand over control.

steps.title: Find the automation candidates
steps.body: Which of these daily activities should you automate with AI? Sorting inbox, writing thank-you notes, creative brainstorming for a new product, summarizing meeting notes, deciding team strategy, scheduling appointments.

questions.label: What is the #1 task you want to automate this week?
How much time per week would that save?

steps.title: Good for automation?
steps.question: Draft a weekly status report based on my project notes.
feedback: Yes. Routine reports are a classic automation win.
Exactly. You check the output, AI does the drafting.

steps.title: Good for automation?
steps.question: Apologize to my partner for forgetting our anniversary.
feedback: AI can help draft something, but personal apologies should come from you.
Right. Some things need your real voice.

questions.question: What is the 80/20 rule of automation?
options: AI does 80%, you do 20% review and adjust

questions.question: If you do the same task three times, you should consider automating it.
Tasks you do repeatedly are called ______ tasks.

steps.title: Day 10 complete — Automation Pro
steps.body: You now know how to spot automation opportunities and build hands-free AI workflows. Tomorrow: using AI to collaborate with others.
